answer: Harvard University was founded on October 28, 1636, in Cambridge, Massachusetts, initially as Harvard College and named after its first benefactor, John Harvard. It is the oldest institution of higher education in the United States.

results.title: This Week in History: Harvard University was founded in 1636
results.snippet: # This Week in History: Harvard University was founded in 1636. Harvard College (now Harvard University) founded, 1636 (Oct. 28). Harvard University was founded at Newtowne (now Cambridge), Massachusetts, on Oct. 28, 1636, just 16 years after the Pilgrims landed at Plymouth. The Massachusetts legislative assembly initially founded Harvard as New College, a school for educating clergymen. The school’s name was changed to Harvard College in 1639, when it was named after the school’s first benefactor, John Harvard. Harvard College opened that same year. Today, a statue of John Harvard stands in Harvard Yard in front of University Hall. More than 250 years after the fire, Harvard now houses the world’s largest university library system. Harvard College was officially renamed Harvard University in 1782 after the founding of its medical school. These include Wadsworth House, which General George Washington used as his first headquarters, and Lowell Hall, named after former Harvard President Abbott Lawrence Lowell.

results.title: History timeline - Harvard University
results.snippet: # A timeline of Harvard's history. Harvard is perhaps best-known because of its enduring history of innovation in education. **1607:** John Harvard, the College’s future namesake and first benefactor, was baptized at St. Saviour’s Church (now Southwark Cathedral), London. The “Great and General Court of the Governor and Company of the Massachusetts Bay in New England” approves £400 for the establishment of “a schoale or colledge” later to be called “Harvard.”. The Overseers purchased the College’s first piece of real estate: a house and an acre of land from Goodman Peyntree. Drafted by Henry Dunster, the Charter of 1650 called for the Harvard Corporation to consist of seven individuals: the President, five Fellows, and a Treasurer. The Charter named the Corporation as the “President and Fellowes of Harvard College” and transferred to them, in “perpetual succession,” the duties of managing the College.

results.title: Harvard University | Notable Alumni, Location, Trump, Degrees, & Facts | Britannica
results.snippet: * Who are the notable alumni of Harvard University? # Harvard University. ### What is Harvard University? Harvard University is the oldest higher-learning institution in the United States, established in 1636. **Harvard University**, oldest institution of higher learning in the United States (founded 1636) and one of the eight Ivy League schools, widely regarded for high academic standards, selectivity in admissions, and social prestige. Radcliffe College, one of the Seven Sisters schools that offered higher education to women at the time of founding, evolved from informal instruction offered to individual women or small groups of women by Harvard University faculty in the 1870s. Although its 1977 agreement with Harvard University called for the integration of select functions, Radcliffe College maintained a separate corporate identity for its property and endowments and continued to offer complementary educational and extracurricular programs for both undergraduate and graduate students, including career programs, a publishing course, and graduate-level workshops and seminars in women’s studies.
